content:
# Philemon Chapter 1 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), pages 223-224 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHM_001_page_001.jpg, PHM_001_page_002.jpg

1. Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother, to Philemon our beloved and fellow worker,

2. and to Apphia our sister, and to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the church in your house:

3. Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

4. I thank my God always, making mention of you in my prayers,

5. hearing of your love and the faith which you have toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the saints,

6. that the fellowship of your faith may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing that is in us for Christ.

7. For I have had much joy and comfort in your love, because the hearts of the saints have been refreshed through you, brother.

8. Therefore, though I have much boldness in Christ to command you what is fitting,

9. for love's sake I rather appeal to you — being such a one as Paul, an old man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus —

10. I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in my bonds, Onesimus,

11. who was formerly useless to you, but now is useful both to you and to me,

12. whom I have sent back to you — him, that is, my very heart —

13. whom I wished to keep with me, that on your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the gospel,

14. but without your consent I wished to do nothing, so that your good deed would not be as by compulsion, but of your own free will.

15. For perhaps he was separated from you for a time for this reason, that you might have him back forever,

16. no longer as a slave, but beyond a slave, a beloved brother — especially to me, but how much more to you, both in the flesh and in the Lord.

17. If then you regard me as a partner, receive him as you would me.

18. But if he has wronged you in anything or owes you anything, charge this to me.

19. I, Paul, write this with my own hand: I will repay it — not to mention to you that you owe me even your own self besides.

20. Yes, brother, let me have benefit from you in the Lord; refresh my heart in Christ.

21. Being confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say.

22. And at the same time, also prepare a lodging for me, for I hope that through your prayers I shall be granted to you.

23. Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, greets you,

24. as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, my fellow workers.

25. The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**Verse 3:** All three translators agree on the substance of this greeting. SOPHIA adopts the standard formal rendering shared by EMET and LOGOS.

**Verse 11:** All three translators preserve the "useless/useful" antithesis. Agreement is strong.

**Verse 15:** All three translators correctly render the divine passive "was separated" and the temporal contrast ("for a time" / "forever"). Agreement is strong.

**Verse 18:** All three translators agree on the core meaning. Minor wording variations are negligible.

**Verse 25:** All three translators agree on the full text including "Christ" and "Amen," following Sinaiticus. Agreement is strong.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 1
- **EMET:** "Paulos, a prisoner of Christ Iesous, and Timotheos the brother, to Philemon the beloved and our fellow-worker"
- **LOGOS:** "Paul, a prisoner of Christ Jesus, and Timothy our brother, to Philemon our beloved and fellow worker"
- **PNEUMA:** "From Paul -- a prisoner for the sake of Christ Jesus -- and from our brother Timothy: To Philemon, our dear friend and fellow worker"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET retains Hellenized names (Paulos, Iesous, Timotheos); LOGOS and PNEUMA use English forms. (2) EMET has "the brother" (article + noun); LOGOS/PNEUMA have "our brother." (3) EMET has "the beloved"; LOGOS has "our beloved"; PNEUMA has "our dear friend." (4) PNEUMA restructures the sentence with dashes and "From...and from" framing, adding "for the sake of" to interpret the genitive.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** English name forms (Paul, Jesus, Timothy) are adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus. "Our brother" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA over EMET's more literal "the brother." "Our beloved" follows LOGOS as the closest formal rendering of the Greek while avoiding PNEUMA's interpretive "dear friend." Standard sentence structure is retained.

### Verse 2
- **EMET:** "Apphia the sister" / "the assembly at your house"
- **LOGOS:** "Apphia our sister" / "the church in your house"
- **PNEUMA:** "Apphia our sister" / "the church that meets in your home"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "the sister"; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "our sister." (2) EMET renders ekklesia as "assembly"; LOGOS/PNEUMA render it "church." (3) PNEUMA adds "that meets in" as interpretive expansion.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Our sister" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. "Church" is adopted over "assembly" as the standard English rendering of ekklesia in the Pauline epistles. "In your house" is preferred over PNEUMA's expansion.

### Verse 4
- **EMET:** "I give-thanks to my God always, making mention of you upon my prayers"
- **LOGOS:** "I thank my God always, making mention of you in my prayers"
- **PNEUMA:** "Every time I pray, I thank God for you, Philemon"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "give-thanks" (hyphenated compound); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "thank." (2) EMET has "upon my prayers"; LOGOS has "in my prayers"; PNEUMA restructures entirely. (3) PNEUMA adds the vocative "Philemon" (not in Greek) and drops "my God" to just "God."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "I thank my God" follows EMET/LOGOS agreement on possessive "my God." "In my prayers" follows LOGOS as natural English. PNEUMA's added vocative "Philemon" is excluded as not present in the source text.

### Verse 5
- **EMET:** "the faith which you have toward the Lord Iesous and unto all the holy-ones"
- **LOGOS:** "the faith which you have toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the saints"
- **PNEUMA:** "the trust you place in the Lord Jesus and extend to all God's people"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "holy-ones"; LOGOS uses "saints"; PNEUMA uses "God's people." (2) PNEUMA renders pistis as "trust" rather than "faith." (3) PNEUMA adds interpretive verbs "place in" and "extend to."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Faith" is retained per EMET/LOGOS agreement. "Saints" follows LOGOS as the standard rendering of hagioi. "Toward" is used for both directional phrases per LOGOS.

### Verse 6
- **EMET:** "so-that the fellowship of your faith might become effective in [the] knowledge of every good [thing] which [is] in us unto Christ"
- **LOGOS:** "that the fellowship of your faith may become effective in the knowledge of every good thing that is in us for Christ"
- **PNEUMA:** "My prayer is that your shared faith will become an active force, deepening your understanding of every good thing we have in Christ"
- **Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA paraphrases "fellowship of your faith" as "shared faith" and "effective" as "an active force." (2) PNEUMA adds "My prayer is that" as framing. (3) PNEUMA renders "knowledge" as "understanding" and "in us" as "we have." (4) EMET has "unto Christ"; LOGOS has "for Christ"; PNEUMA has "in Christ."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "The fellowship of your faith may become effective" follows EMET/LOGOS agreement on the formal structure. "In the knowledge of every good thing that is in us for Christ" follows LOGOS, which preserves the Sinaiticus reading "in us" (EN HMIN) while rendering natural English. Manuscript verification confirms EN HMIN on page 223.

### Verse 7
- **EMET:** "I had much joy and comfort upon your love, because the inward-parts of the holy-ones have been refreshed through you"
- **LOGOS:** "I have had much joy and comfort in your love, because the hearts of the saints have been refreshed through you"
- **PNEUMA:** "Your love has brought me tremendous joy and encouragement, brother, because you have refreshed the hearts of God's people"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "inward-parts" (literal splanchna); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "hearts." (2) EMET uses "holy-ones"; LOGOS uses "saints"; PNEUMA uses "God's people." (3) EMET has "upon your love"; LOGOS has "in your love"; PNEUMA restructures. (4) EMET has "comfort"; LOGOS has "comfort"; PNEUMA has "encouragement." (5) PNEUMA places "brother" mid-sentence; EMET/LOGOS place it at end.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Hearts" is adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus as the natural English equivalent of splanchna. "Saints" follows LOGOS. "Comfort" follows EMET/LOGOS agreement. "In your love" follows LOGOS. "Brother" placed at end per EMET/LOGOS. Manuscript confirms ΧΑΡΑΝ (joy) on page 223.

### Verse 8
- **EMET:** "having much boldness in Christ to command you the [thing which is] fitting"
- **LOGOS:** "though I have much boldness in Christ to command you what is fitting"
- **PNEUMA:** "even though I would be well within my rights in Christ to tell you what you ought to do"
- **Divergences:** (1) PNEUMA paraphrases "boldness" as "well within my rights" and "command" as "tell." (2) PNEUMA renders "fitting" as "what you ought to do." (3) EMET uses a participial construction; LOGOS/PNEUMA use concessive clauses.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Though I have much boldness in Christ to command you what is fitting" follows LOGOS, preserving both "boldness" and "command" as accurate renderings while providing a readable English concessive clause.

### Verse 9
- **EMET:** "on-account-of love rather I appeal, being such-a-one as Paulos, an old-man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Iesous"
- **LOGOS:** "for love's sake I rather appeal to you -- being such a one as Paul, an old man, and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus"
- **PNEUMA:** "I would rather make my appeal on the basis of love. So here I am -- Paul, an old man now, and on top of that a prisoner for Christ Jesus"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "on-account-of love"; LOGOS has "for love's sake"; PNEUMA has "on the basis of love." (2) PNEUMA adds "So here I am" and "on top of that" as interpretive expansions. (3) All three correctly render presbytes as "old man" per Sinaiticus. (4) PNEUMA has "for Christ Jesus" (interpreting genitive); EMET/LOGOS have "of Christ Jesus."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "For love's sake" follows LOGOS as elegant and accurate. "Of Christ Jesus" retained per EMET/LOGOS. Manuscript verification on page 223 confirms ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΗΣ (old man), not ΠΡΕΣΒΕΥΤΗΣ (ambassador). All three translators agree on this reading.

### Verse 10
- **EMET:** "I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in the bonds, Onesimos"
- **LOGOS:** "I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in my bonds, Onesimus"
- **PNEUMA:** "I am appealing to you on behalf of my child Onesimus, whose father in the faith I became while here in chains"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "in the bonds"; LOGOS has "in my bonds"; PNEUMA has "while here in chains." (2) PNEUMA restructures to "whose father in the faith I became," adding "in the faith" (not in Greek). (3) PNEUMA uses present tense "am appealing"; EMET/LOGOS use simple present "appeal." (4) EMET has "Onesimos"; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "Onesimus."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in my bonds, Onesimus" follows LOGOS. "My bonds" is slightly more natural than EMET's "the bonds." PNEUMA's "father in the faith" is an interpretive addition excluded from the consensus. English name form "Onesimus" per LOGOS/PNEUMA.

### Verse 12
- **EMET:** "whom I sent-back to you, him, that is, my own inward-parts"
- **LOGOS:** "whom I have sent back to you -- him, that is, my very heart"
- **PNEUMA:** "I am sending him back to you -- and with him goes my very heart"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET uses "inward-parts"; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "heart" for splanchna. (2) EMET uses aorist "sent-back"; LOGOS uses perfect "have sent back"; PNEUMA uses present "am sending." (3) PNEUMA adds "and with him goes" as interpretive expansion. (4) EMET has "my own inward-parts"; LOGOS has "my very heart."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "My very heart" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus. "Have sent back" follows LOGOS for English present perfect, appropriate for an epistolary context. The appositive structure "him, that is, my very heart" follows EMET/LOGOS.

### Verse 13
- **EMET:** "whom I was wishing to retain with myself, so-that on your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the good-news"
- **LOGOS:** "whom I wished to keep with me, that on your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the gospel"
- **PNEUMA:** "I would have loved to keep him here with me, so that he could take your place in serving me during my imprisonment for the gospel"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "retain with myself"; LOGOS has "keep with me"; PNEUMA has "keep him here with me." (2) EMET renders euangelion as "good-news"; LOGOS/PNEUMA use "gospel." (3) PNEUMA paraphrases "on your behalf he might serve me" as "take your place in serving me." (4) PNEUMA renders "bonds of the gospel" as "imprisonment for the gospel."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Wished to keep with me" follows LOGOS. "Gospel" adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus. "On your behalf he might serve me in the bonds of the gospel" follows LOGOS, preserving the Greek structure.

### Verse 14
- **EMET:** "apart-from your purpose I wished to do nothing, so-that your good [deed] might not be as according-to compulsion but according-to willingness"
- **LOGOS:** "without your consent I wished to do nothing, so that your good deed would not be as by compulsion, but of your own will"
- **PNEUMA:** "I did not want to do anything without your agreement, so that your act of kindness would be freely given, not forced"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "purpose"; LOGOS has "consent"; PNEUMA has "agreement." (2) EMET has "willingness"; LOGOS has "your own will"; PNEUMA has "freely given." (3) PNEUMA restructures to active voice. (4) PNEUMA renders "good deed" as "act of kindness."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Consent" follows LOGOS as accurate for gnome. "Your good deed" follows EMET/LOGOS. "Of your own free will" synthesizes LOGOS's "of your own will" with added clarity. The phrase "free will" captures the hekousion of the Greek.

### Verse 16
- **EMET:** "above a slave, a brother beloved, especially to me, and how much more to you, both in [the] flesh and in [the] Lord"
- **LOGOS:** "beyond a slave, a beloved brother -- especially to me, but how much more to you, both in the flesh and in the Lord"
- **PNEUMA:** "as something far better: a beloved brother. He is already very dear to me; how much more will he be to you, both as a person and as a brother in the Lord"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "above a slave"; LOGOS has "beyond a slave"; PNEUMA has "something far better." (2) PNEUMA splits into two sentences and adds "already very dear." (3) PNEUMA renders "in the flesh" as "as a person" and "in the Lord" as "as a brother in the Lord." (4) EMET has "and how much more"; LOGOS has "but how much more"; PNEUMA has "how much more will he be."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Beyond a slave" follows LOGOS for hyper doulou. "A beloved brother" follows LOGOS word order. "But how much more" follows LOGOS. "Both in the flesh and in the Lord" follows EMET/LOGOS agreement against PNEUMA's paraphrase.

### Verse 17
- **EMET:** "If therefore you hold me [as] a partner, receive him as me"
- **LOGOS:** "If then you regard me as a partner, receive him as you would me"
- **PNEUMA:** "So if you consider me your partner, welcome him as you would welcome me"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "hold me as"; LOGOS has "regard me as"; PNEUMA has "consider me." (2) EMET has "receive him as me" (compressed); LOGOS has "receive him as you would me"; PNEUMA has "welcome him as you would welcome me." (3) PNEUMA uses "welcome" rather than "receive" for proslabou.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Regard me as a partner" follows LOGOS. "Receive him as you would me" follows LOGOS, preserving proslabou as "receive" per EMET/LOGOS while adding "you would" for English clarity.

### Verse 19
- **EMET:** "I, Paulos, wrote with my own hand: I will repay -- in-order-that I may not say to you that you also owe me yourself besides"
- **LOGOS:** "I, Paul, write this with my own hand: I will repay it -- not to mention to you that you owe me even your own self besides"
- **PNEUMA:** "I, Paul, am writing this with my own hand: I will pay it back. And I will not even mention that you owe me your very self"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "wrote" (aorist); LOGOS has "write"; PNEUMA has "am writing." (2) EMET has "in-order-that I may not say"; LOGOS has "not to mention"; PNEUMA has "I will not even mention." (3) PNEUMA has "pay it back" vs. "repay." (4) EMET has "yourself"; LOGOS has "your own self"; PNEUMA has "your very self."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Write this with my own hand" follows LOGOS for present tense epistolary convention. "I will repay it" follows LOGOS. "Not to mention to you that you owe me even your own self besides" follows LOGOS, preserving the paralipsis while keeping "besides" (prosopheileis).

### Verse 20
- **EMET:** "may I have profit from you in [the] Lord; refresh my inward-parts in Christ"
- **LOGOS:** "let me have benefit from you in the Lord; refresh my heart in Christ"
- **PNEUMA:** "let me get some benefit from you in the Lord! Refresh my heart in Christ"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "may I have profit" (optative); LOGOS has "let me have benefit"; PNEUMA has "let me get some benefit." (2) EMET has "inward-parts"; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "heart" for splanchna.
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Let me have benefit" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA majority for the optative onaimen. "Heart" adopted per LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus for splanchna.

### Verse 21
- **EMET:** "Having been persuaded of your obedience, I wrote to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say"
- **LOGOS:** "Being confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say"
- **PNEUMA:** "I am writing to you confident that you will do what I ask -- and I know you will go even beyond what I am asking"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "having been persuaded"; LOGOS has "being confident"; PNEUMA has "confident." (2) EMET has "wrote" (aorist); LOGOS has "write"; PNEUMA has "am writing." (3) PNEUMA restructures and adds "what I ask" / "what I am asking" for "what I say."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Being confident" follows LOGOS for the participle pepoithos. "I write to you" follows LOGOS for epistolary present. "Even beyond what I say" follows EMET/LOGOS agreement.

### Verse 22
- **EMET:** "prepare for me a lodging"
- **LOGOS:** "prepare a lodging for me"
- **PNEUMA:** "get a guest room ready for me"
- **Divergences:** (1) Word order differs between EMET and LOGOS. (2) PNEUMA paraphrases "lodging" (xenia) as "guest room" and "prepare" as "get...ready."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Prepare a lodging for me" follows LOGOS word order. "Lodging" retained per EMET/LOGOS over PNEUMA's "guest room."

### Verse 23
- **EMET:** "Epaphras greets you, my fellow-captive in Christ Iesous"
- **LOGOS:** "Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, greets you"
- **PNEUMA:** "Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, sends you his greetings"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET has "fellow-captive"; LOGOS/PNEUMA have "fellow prisoner" for sunaichmalotos. (2) Sentence structure differs: EMET places "greets you" before the appositive; LOGOS/PNEUMA place it after. (3) PNEUMA expands "greets" to "sends you his greetings."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** "Fellow prisoner" follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. Appositive structure follows LOGOS/PNEUMA placement. "Greets you" follows EMET/LOGOS over PNEUMA's expansion.

### Verse 24
- **EMET:** "Markos, Aristarchos, Demas, Loukas, my fellow-workers"
- **LOGOS:** "as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke, my fellow workers"
- **PNEUMA:** "and so do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke -- my co-workers"
- **Divergences:** (1) EMET uses Hellenized names (Markos, Aristarchos, Loukas); LOGOS/PNEUMA use English forms. (2) EMET lacks connective; LOGOS has "as do"; PNEUMA has "and so do." (3) PNEUMA uses "co-workers" instead of "fellow workers."
- **SOPHIA's resolution:** English name forms per LOGOS/PNEUMA. "As do" follows LOGOS. "Fellow workers" per EMET/LOGOS agreement.

---
*SOPHIA: I have examined all twenty-five verses of Paul's letter to Philemon across the three translations, verified key readings against Codex Sinaiticus pages 223-224, and produced this consensus. The manuscript is clear and well-preserved for this epistle. Key Sinaiticus-specific readings preserved in the consensus include: "the sister" / "our sister" for Apphia (v.2), "in us" rather than "in you" (v.6), "joy" (CHARAN) not "grace" (v.7), and "old man" (PRESBYTES) not "ambassador" (v.9). The text concludes with AMEN per Sinaiticus.*

-- SOPHIA
